chunks.text: My father lost his right leg in a traffic accident when he was twenty - five.
From that time on, a crippled life began.
The first few years were despairing.
Adapting to a prosthetic leg was a bitter process, and just walking took hours.
My father, having received such a blow, often resented himself alone at night.
But my father was a strong person.
He did not yield to depress.
He accepted his disability and made up his mind to build a new life.
He began his studies at an accounting institute.
In order to qualify for a profession he could do while seated
three years later he earned the license of a certified accountant.
After that
he went independent as a consultant in the economics of small and medium businesses.
With exact calculation and sincere work, he won the trust of many clients.
He married my mother, and my sister and I were born.
Even with a prosthetic leg, my father played with us in the park.
Riding a bicycle was unlikely, but he could swim with us in the pool.
My father often said
" The most important thing in life is not what you have lost
but what you make from what remains.
At sixty, my father retired.
Today, he works voluntary at a local organization supporting people with disabilities.
He gives advice from his own experience to young people who have gone through the same.
To lose a part of one's body is a terrible experience.
But what my father taught me is that " to lose is also the beginning of rebirth.
Hardship deepens a person.
My father's quiet strength was born from the experience of losing a leg.
